preamble: The shift from the glottal fricative [h] to the voiced velar stop [g] is a form of fortition combined with place shift (glottal → velar) and manner shift (fricative → stop). It is typologically uncommon as a direct unconditioned change, but is best understood through the intermediate stage h > x > ɣ > g, a pathway attested in several language histories. The historical relationship between [h], [x], [ɣ], and [g] is close: [h] is frequently a reduced allophone of [x], and [ɣ] is the voiced counterpart, which can further fortify to [g] in certain phonological environments, notably post-nasal position. The clearest morphological attestation is in the Grammatischer Wechsel of Old English and Gothic strong verbs, where Verner's Law created paradigmatic alternations between [h]/[x] and [g] (from earlier *ɣ) — for example, OE slēan (to strike, infinitive) versus slōgon (they struck, past plural). In Shambala (Northeast Bantu, Tanzania), post-nasal fortition drives both [h] and [ɣ] to surface as [g] in a regular conditioned environment.

examples.note: The alternation reflects Verner's Law: Proto-Germanic *slaxan (with stress on root) gave *slōgun via voicing *x > *ɣ in an unstressed syllable, which later merged with /g/ in Old English. This is a morphological h/g alternation, not a regular unconditioned sound law.
Another strong verb class 2 example showing the h~g paradigmatic alternation in Old English.

In Shambala (a Northeast Bantu language of Tanzania), post-nasal fortition changes [h] and [ɣ] to [g]. This is a regular, conditioned sound change in this language, cited in comparative Bantu phonology literature.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction, 3rd ed. MIT Press.
Maddieson, I. (2019). The sounds of the Bantu languages. In M. Van de Velde et al. (eds.), The Bantu Languages, 2nd ed. Routledge.
